Styles › Brutalism

1950s – 1980s · Europe → Global

Brutalism

Bold, massive, sculptural — exposed raw concrete.

  • Overview
  • Design Philosophy
  • Visual Characteristics
  • Materials
  • Color Palette
  • Façade Guide
  • Lighting
  • Building Types
  • Design Checklist
  • Common Mistakes
  • AI Prompts
  • Gallery
  • Related

01 — Overview

Overview

Definition. Brutalism is an architecture of bold, sculptural, monumental masses in exposed raw concrete (béton brut), expressing structure, weight and the texture of construction.

Historical background. Born in post-war reconstruction, it offered an economical, durable, 'honest' architecture, showing the imprint of its own timber shuttering.

Origin. Britain and France, mid-1950s.

Timeline

1952

Unité d'Habitation, Marseille

1955

'New Brutalism' coined

1968

Boston City Hall

1976

Barbican Estate, London

Key Architects

Le CorbusierAlison & Peter SmithsonPaul RudolphErnő Goldfinger

Influential Projects

Unité d'Habitation

Le Corbusier · 1952
Marseille, France

Barbican Estate

Chamberlin, Powell & Bon · 1976
London, UK

Boston City Hall

Kallmann McKinnell & Knowles · 1968
Boston, USA

02 — Design Philosophy

Design Philosophy

Core Principles

Truth to materialsStructural honestyMonumentalityExpression of massFunction as form

The Thinking

Brutalism refuses disguise. Structure and circulation are made visible and celebrated; the building is an honest sculpted object of weight and texture.

Values It Represents

IntegrityPermanenceSocial purposeDirectness

Why It Emerged

From austerity and idealism — an ethical, affordable, enduring architecture for housing, universities and government.

03 — Visual Characteristics

Visual Characteristics

MassingHeavy, sculptural, often top-heavy volumes; bold cantilevers casting deep shadow.
Form LanguageChunky and sculptural — towers, megastructures, interlocking blocks.
CompositionRepetition of strong modular units; dramatic solid/void contrast.
ProportionMassive and weighty; oversized to read at urban scale.
GeometryAngular and faceted; occasional powerful curves (ramps).
RhythmInsistent repetition of cellular units — balconies, fins.
SymmetryOften symmetrical and monumental, rugged in surface.
ScaleMonumental, civic, deliberately imposing.
Roof TypesFlat, with expressive rooftop plant and sculptural elements.
Window ProportionDeep-set recessed openings; strong shadow.
EntranceElevated, processional — reached by ramps or bridges.
Façade ArticulationBoard-marked or bush-hammered concrete; formwork texture as ornament.

04 — Materials

Materials

Béton brut (raw concrete)

Cast in place, left exposed, carrying its formwork texture.

Board-formed concrete

Timber shuttering imprints a warm grain into the concrete.

Brick

Warmth and human scale alongside concrete in housing.

Exposed aggregate

Rugged texture that weathers and reveals substance.

05 — Color Palette

Color Palette

Sombre, powerful, elemental — relieved by occasional bold accents.

Primary

Raw Concrete
#9a958c
Shadow Grey
#6c685f

Accent

Oxide Red
#9c4a32
Bottle Green
#3c5045

Material Colors

Weathered Brown
#7a6a55
Aggregate
#8f8a7e

Typical Combinations

  • +Monolithic concrete greys
  • +Concrete + brick + bold primary accents

06 — Façade Design Guide

Façade Design Guide

Window CompositionDeep reveals and recessed glazing for strong shadow; group into cellular modules.
EmphasisVertical service towers against horizontal balcony decks.
Shading DevicesThe mass shades itself; deep balconies and fins as sun control.
BalconiesRobust projecting concrete balconies as the rhythmic device.
FramesRecessed and secondary — the structure dominates.
OpeningsRepetitive cellular openings; vary to express programme.
Architectural DetailsBoard-marks, drips, expressed beams and waterspouts.
OrnamentationNone applied — texture and structural rhythm provide richness.

07 — Lighting

Lighting

Natural Lighting Strategy

Sculptural mass creates dramatic self-shadowing on the exterior.

Artificial Lighting Concept

Conceal sources; graze concrete to reveal texture and weight.

Night Façade Appearance

Uplighting and grazing make the concrete monumental; recesses read as voids.

Recommendations

Warm grazing 2700–3000K to counter concrete's coolness; emphasise texture.

08 — Typical Building Types

Typical Building Types

Where this style performs best:

Civic & governmentUniversitiesHousingCultural centresCar parksLibraries

09 — Design Checklist

Design Checklist

Tap an item to mark it complete — saved to your browser.

  • Express raw concrete left exposed
  • Use board-formed or textured surfaces
  • Compose bold sculptural masses
  • Recess windows deeply for shadow
  • Make ramps, bridges and towers visible
  • Repeat strong cellular units
  • Keep palette monolithic; accent sparingly
  • Detail honest drips and expressed beams

10 — Common Mistakes

Common Mistakes

Elements that contradict the style — what to avoid:

  • ✕Painting or cladding over the concrete
  • ✕Thin, lightweight forms lacking mass
  • ✕Decorative add-ons or historical reference
  • ✕Shallow flush windows with no shadow
  • ✕Pastel colours undermining the gravity

11 — AI Prompt Examples

AI Prompt Examples

Optimised for Midjourney, ChatGPT image tools or Claude — exterior renders. Tap copy to use.

Exterior

Brutalist civic building exterior, raw board-formed concrete, bold cantilevered sculptural mass, deep-set windows, dramatic shadow, monumental, overcast sky, wide-angle architectural photography --ar 3:2 --style raw

Façade Concept

Brutalist concrete façade, board-marked texture, repetitive cellular balcony modules, deep reveals, strong raking light, monochrome, high detail

Night View

Brutalist tower exterior at night, concrete grazed by warm uplight revealing formwork, deep recesses in shadow, moody architectural night photography

Aerial View

Aerial of a Brutalist megastructure, interlocking concrete blocks and elevated walkways, flat roofs, hard shadows, grey palette, drone view

Street Perspective

Street view looking up at a Brutalist housing block, projecting concrete balconies, ramp entrance, dramatic perspective, documentary photography

Masterplan

Brutalist masterplan, megastructure with elevated decks, repetitive slabs, raw concrete, bold geometry, axonometric, monochrome
